context.pr_title: Fix issue with > in pip cmd causing file creation
context.pr_body:
### Problem Summary

When starting the Web UI, the `install.py` script reads `requirements.txt`. If a package version uses ">", it is treated as a redirection in `pip install`, creating an unintended file.

### Steps to Reproduce

Running `webui.bat` or `webui.sh` creates a file named "=4.8.0" due to "opencv-python>=4.8.0" in `requirements.txt` being misinterpreted.

### Fix

I enclosed the package and version specification in double quotes in `install.py`.

agents.name: analysis_agent
agents.system_prompt:
You are an Analysis Agent specialized in Pull Request analysis.

## Project Context (MASCA Analysis)
## Project Analysis

### README
# ControlNet for Stable Diffusion WebUI

The WebUI extension for ControlNet and other injection-based SD controls.
![image](https://github.com/Mikubill/sd-webui-controlnet/assets/20929282/261f9a50-ba9c-472f-b398-fced61929c4a)

This extension is for AUTOMATIC1111's [Stable Diffusion web UI](https://github.com/AUTOMATIC1111/stable-diffusion-webui), allows the Web UI to add [ControlNet](https://github.com/lllyasviel/ControlNet) to the original Stable Diffusion model to generate images. The addition is on-the-fly, the merging is not required.

# News

- [2024-05-19] 🔥[v1.1.449] Anyline Preprocessor & MistoLine SDXL model [Discussion thread: https://github.com/Mikubill/sd-webui-controlnet/discussions/2907]
- [2024-05-04] 🔥[v1.1.447] PuLID [Discussion thread: https://github.com/Mikubill/sd-webui-controlnet/discussions/2841]
- [2024-04-30] 🔥[v1.1.446] Effective region mask supported for ControlNet/IPAdapter [Discussion thread: https://github.com/Mikubill/sd-webui-controlnet/discussions/2831]
- [2024-04-27] 🔥ControlNet-lllite Normal Dsine released [Discussion thread: https://github.com/Mikubill/sd-webui-controlnet/discussions/2813]
- [2024-04-19] 🔥[v1.1.445] IPAdapter advanced weight [Instant Style] [Discussion thread: https://github.com/Mikubill/sd-webui-controlnet/discussions/2770]
- [2024-04-17] 🔥[v1.1.444] Marigold depth preprocessor [Discussion thread: https://github.com/Mikubill/sd-webui-controlnet/discussions/2760]
- [2024-04-15] 🔥ControlNet++ models released [Discussion thread: https://github.com/Mikubill/sd-webui-controlnet/discussions/2778]
- [2024-04-13] 🔥TTPLanet_SDXL_Controlnet_Tile_Realistic v2 released [[Civitai Page](https://civitai.com/models/330313/ttplanetsdxlcontrolnettilerealistic)]
- [2024-03-31] 🔥[v1.1.443] IP-Adapter CLIP mask and ip-adapter-auto preprocessor [Discussion thread: https://github.com/Mikubill/sd-webui-controlnet/discussions/2723]
- [2024-03-20] 🔥IPAdapter Composition [Discussion thread: https://github.com/Mikubill/sd-webui-controlnet/discussions/2781]

# Installation

1. Open "Extensions" tab.
2. Open "Install from URL" tab in the tab.
3. Enter `https://github.com/Mikubill/sd-webui-controlnet.git` to "URL for extension's git repository".
4. Press "Install" button.
5. Wait for 5 seconds, and you will see the message "Installed into stable-diffusion-webui\extensions\sd-webui-controlnet. Use Installed tab to restart".
6. Go to "Installed" tab, click "Check for updates", and then click "Apply and restart UI". (The next time you can also use these buttons to update ControlNet.)
7. Completely restart A1111 webui including your terminal. (If you do not know what is a "terminal", you can reboot your computer to achieve the same effect.)
8. Download models (see below).
9. After you put models in the correct folder, you may need to refresh to see the models. The refresh button is right to your "Model" dropdown.

# Download Models
You can find all download links here: https://github.com/Mikubill/sd-webui-controlnet/wiki/Model-d


## Your Task
When you receive a PR title and body, you must:

1. **Analyze the request**: Understand what the PR wants to achieve
2. **Explore the code**: Use tools to navigate base_project and find relevant files/functions
3. **Identify targets**: Determine exactly which files and functions need to be modified
4. **Optimize MASCA**: Produce a version of the MASCA analysis focused on the specific PR

## Available Tools
- `read_base_project_file(file_path)`: Read a file from the project
- `list_base_project_directory(directory, pattern, recursive)`: List files/folders

## Required Output
You must produce a structured output with:
- `pr_title`: PR title
- `pr_body`: PR body
- `masca_optimized`: MASCA optimized for this specific PR
- `files_to_modify`: List of files to modify with motivation
- `functions_to_modify`: List of functions to modify with motivation
- `analysis_summary`: Summary of your analysis

## Guidelines
- Explore the code before drawing conclusions
- Search for files with names relevant to the PR
- Identify specific functions, not just generic files
- Be precise with paths (relative to base_project)
- For function names use the format "ClassName.method_name" for methods
agents.input_prompt:
Analyze this Pull Request:

## Title
Fix issue with > in pip cmd causing file creation

## Description
### Problem Summary

When starting the Web UI, the `install.py` script reads `requirements.txt`. If a package version uses ">", it is treated as a redirection in `pip install`, creating an unintended file.

### Steps to Reproduce

Running `webui.bat` or `webui.sh` creates a file named "=4.8.0" due to "opencv-python>=4.8.0" in `requirements.txt` being misinterpreted.

### Fix

I enclosed the package and version specification in double quotes in `install.py`.

Explore the source code to identify the files and functions to modify.

arguments.reason: Find where requirements.txt is read and pip install command built
arguments.expected_information: Identify function(s) that read requirements.txt, build pip install commands, and where quoting should be applied

tool_calls.result:
### File Overview
This file (install.py) installs Python dependencies for the project. It reads a requirements file, decides whether packages must be installed/updated, and runs pip install commands (via the external launch.run_pip helper); it also installs onnxruntime/onnxruntime-gpu, attempts to install prebuilt wheels, and removes a legacy submodule directory.

### Architecture
- Top-level globals: repo_root and main_req_file point to the repository root and requirements.txt.
- Utility helpers: comparable_version, get_installed_version, extract_base_package for version comparison, metadata lookup, and parsing package strings.
- Installer functions: install_requirements, install_onnxruntime, try_install_from_wheel, try_install_insight_face, try_remove_legacy_submodule implement specific installation behaviors.
- External interface: All actual pip invocations are delegated to launch.run_pip and presence checks use launch.is_installed.
- Execution sequence (bottom of file): install_requirements(main_req_file) -> install_onnxruntime() -> try_install_insight_face() -> multiple try_install_from_wheel(...) -> try_remove_legacy_submodule().

### Functions & Classes
- repo_root — Path: repo_root = Path(__file__).parent. Points to the directory containing this file; used to locate requirements.txt and the legacy submodule path.
- main_req_file — Path: main_req_file = repo_root / "requirements.txt". The requirements file that is passed into install_requirements at module execution.

- comparable_version(version: str) -> Tuple:
  - Splits a version string on "." and returns a tuple for lexicographic comparison (e.g., "1.2.3" -> ("1","2","3")).
  - Importance: used to compare installed vs required versions when deciding whether to install or upgrade.

- get_installed_version(package: str) -> Optional[str]:
  - Uses importlib.metadata.version(package) to get the installed version string or returns None on exception.
  - Importance: determines current installation state so installs/upgrades are only attempted when necessary.

- extract_base_package(package_string: str) -> str:
  - Splits package_string at "@git" and returns the part before it.
  - Importance: used to derive the package name to pass to launch.is_installed when requirements lines contain a git URL (so presence checks work).

- install_requirements(req_file):
  - Opens req_file and iterates over each line as package (strips whitespace).
  - For each package line it performs different behaviors depending on whether the line contains "==", ">=", "<=", or otherwise:
    - "==" case: splits to package_name, package_version, compares with get_installed_version; if different, runs launch.run_pip(f"install -U {package}", <message>).
    - ">=" case: splits to package_name, package_version; if not installed or installed_version < required_version (using comparable_version) then run launch.run_pip(f"install -U {package}", <message>).
    - "<=" case: splits to package_name, package_version; if not installed or installed_version > required_version then run launch.run_pip(f"install {package_name}=={package_version}", <message>).
    - otherwise: uses extract_base_package(package) and if not launch.is_installed(...) then run launch.run_pip(f"install {package}", <message>).
  - Exceptions while processing a package print a warning.
  - Importance: This is the primary function that reads requirements.txt (main_req_file) and builds the pip commands that will be executed. This is the most relevant function for finding where requirements.txt is read and where pip commands are composed.

- install_onnxruntime():
  - Checks launch.is_installed for "onnxruntime" and "onnxruntime-gpu". If neither installed, imports torch.cuda as cuda and picks 'onnxruntime-gpu' if cuda.is_available() else 'onnxruntime', then calls launch.run_pip(f'install {onnxruntime}', <message>).
  - Importance: Conditional installation of GPU/CPU onnxruntime; also builds a pip command using an interpolated string without additional quoting.

- try_install_from_wheel(pkg_name: str, wheel_url: str, version: Optional[str] = None):
  - Gets current_version via get_installed_version(pkg_name).
  - If current version exists and version argument is None, returns (no-op), or if version provided and current_version >= version returns (no-op).
  - Otherwise calls launch.run_pip(f"install -U {wheel_url}", <message>) in a try/except where exceptions are printed and warn.
  - Importance: Constructs pip install commands for wheel URLs and contains logic to avoid unnecessary installs when version is satisfied.

- try_install_insight_face():
  - If "insightface" already installed, returns.
  - Constructs a wheel_url from environment INSIGHTFACE_WHEEL or default_win_wheel. Determines system, architecture, python_version and if conditions match attempts launch.run_pip(f"install {wheel_url}", <message>) otherwise prints a message asking manual pip install.
  - Importance: Another place that builds pip install from a URL; attempts to use prebuilt wheels to avoid building from source.

- try_remove_legacy_submodule():
  - Attempts to remove repo_root / "annotator" / "hand_refiner_portable" using shutil.rmtree if it exists; prints errors on failure.
  - Importance: Housekeeping to remove a legacy submodule directory after installations.

- Module-level execution (bottom of file):
  - Calls install_requirements(main_req_file)
  - Calls install_onnxruntime()
  - Calls try_install_insight_face()
  - Calls try_install_from_wheel("handrefinerportable", wheel_url=os.environ.get("HANDREFINER_WHEEL", ...), version="2024.2.12.0")
  - Calls try_install_from_wheel("depth_anything", wheel_url=os.environ.get("DEPTH_ANYTHING_WHEEL", ...))
  - Calls try_install_from_wheel("dsine", wheel_url=os.environ.get("DSINE_WHEEL", ...))
  - Calls try_remove_legacy_submodule()
  - Importance: Shows the runtime ordering; the requirements file is read immediately when this module is executed.

### Relevance to Agent's Goal
- Where requirements.txt is read:
  - The file main_req_file = repo_root / "requirements.txt" defines the requirements file.
  - install_requirements(req_file) opens req_file and iterates the lines; the module-level call install_requirements(main_req_file) executes this logic. This is the exact place requirements.txt is read.

- Where pip install commands are built:
  - All calls to launch.run_pip build pip commands using f-strings. Key occurrences:
    - In install_requirements:
      - f"install -U {package}" (for "==" and ">=" cases)
      - f"install {package_name}=={package_version}" (for "<=" case)
      - f"install {package}" (for non-version lines)
    - In install_onnxruntime:
      - f'install {onnxruntime}'
    - In try_install_from_wheel:
      - f"install -U {wheel_url}"
    - In try_install_insight_face:
      - f"install {wheel_url}"
  - These are the exact string constructions that are handed to launch.run_pip.

- Where quoting should be applied:
  - The constructed pip command strings interpolate package, package_name, package_version, and wheel_url directly into f-strings with no additional quoting or escaping. If any package line or wheel_url can contain characters that require shell quoting (spaces, ampersands, parentheses, shell metacharacters, or complex requirement specifiers) or if launch.run_pip ultimately executes a shell command, these are the spots to apply quoting/escaping:
    - f"install -U {package}"
    - f"install {package}"
    - f"install {package_name}=={package_version}"
    - f"install -U {wheel_url}"
    - f"install {wheel_url}"
  - Additionally, extract_base_package(package_string) handles splitting "@git" but other VCS/URL forms may require careful handling before passing to launch.run_pip.

- Other notes relevant to modification:
  - launch.run_pip and launch.is_installed are external helpers; how quoting needs to be applied depends on launch.run_pip's implementation (whether it runs pip via subprocess with a list of args or via a shell).
  - Version comparison uses comparable_version which splits on "." but treats segments as strings (not numeric); that may affect comparison semantics for multi-digit segments (e.g., "10" vs "2") — relevant if deciding whether to upgrade.
  - All pip commands are assembled at the call-sites listed above; to add safe quoting/escaping, modify these exact f-strings or change launch.run_pip to accept arguments as lists and handle escaping internally.

output.pr_title: Fix issue with > in pip cmd causing file creation
output.pr_body:
### Problem Summary

When starting the Web UI, the `install.py` script reads `requirements.txt`. If a package version uses ">", it is treated as a redirection in `pip install`, creating an unintended file.

### Steps to Reproduce

Running `webui.bat` or `webui.sh` creates a file named "=4.8.0" due to "opencv-python>=4.8.0" in `requirements.txt` being misinterpreted.

### Fix

I enclosed the package and version specification in double quotes in `install.py`.
output.masca_optimized:
Goal: Prevent shell redirection when install.py constructs pip commands by ensuring any interpolated package/version/wheel strings are quoted (so characters like '>' are not interpreted by the shell).

Key observations from the codebase:
- install.py reads requirements.txt via install_requirements(main_req_file) and builds pip commands using f-strings such as:
  - f"install -U {package}"
  - f"install {package}"
  - f"install {package_name}=={package_version}"
- Other places building pip commands: install_onnxruntime(), try_install_from_wheel(), try_install_insight_face().
- launch.run_pip is the executor for these command strings (implementation not in this extension). It appears to accept command strings and likely invokes pip in a shell context; therefore shell metacharacters in interpolated values can cause issues.

Minimal safe fix (scope of this PR): Modify install.py to quote any interpolated package or URL when building pip command strings. This keeps behavior identical for normal values but prevents shell redirection for strings containing '>', '<', spaces, or other metacharacters.

Exact modifications to make in install.py:
- install_requirements(): change all pip command constructions to put the package/specifier in double quotes, e.g.:
  - from f"install -U {package}" to f'install -U "{package}"'
  - from f"install {package_name}=={package_version}" to f'install "{package_name}=={package_version}"'
  - from f"install {package}" to f'install "{package}"'
- install_onnxruntime(): quote the chosen onnxruntime package name: f'install "{onnxruntime}"'
- try_install_from_wheel(): quote the wheel_url: f'install -U "{wheel_url}"'
- try_install_insight_face(): quote the wheel_url: f'install "{wheel_url}"'

Rationale: Quoting at the call sites is a low-risk, localized change that prevents shell interpretation of comparison operators (e.g., '>') coming from requirements.txt. A more robust but broader alternative would be to change launch.run_pip to accept an argv list and invoke subprocess without shell=True, but that's out of scope here.

Tests & verification:
- Run webui.bat / webui.sh before/after the change with requirements containing a specifier like "opencv-python>=4.8.0" and verify no file named "=4.8.0" is created.
- Confirm pip commands still install the intended packages (manual or CI test of install.py behavior).

Files to change: only install.py. Functions to change: install_requirements, install_onnxruntime, try_install_from_wheel, try_install_insight_face.

This is a minimal, targeted fix that addresses the reported reproduction path.

files_to_modify.reason: This module reads requirements.txt and builds pip install command strings with f-strings. Unquoted package/version strings containing '>' are interpreted by the shell as redirections, causing unintended file creation. Applying quoting here prevents shell interpretation.

functions_to_modify.reason: This function constructs pip commands from lines in requirements.txt (e.g., f"install -U {package}", f"install {package}", f"install {package_name}=={package_version}"). Quoting the package/specifier will prevent '>' or other shell metacharacters from being interpreted by the shell.
This function builds a pip command using the chosen onnxruntime package name (f'install {onnxruntime}'). While less likely to contain '>' operators, quoting keeps it safe and consistent.
This function runs pip with a wheel URL via f"install -U {wheel_url}". Quoting the URL avoids shell interpretation of any special characters.
This function may call pip with a wheel URL (f"install {wheel_url}"). Quoting the wheel URL prevents shell metacharacter issues and matches the other fixes.

output.analysis_summary: The root cause is unquoted interpolation of package specifiers and URLs into pip command strings in install.py. The minimal and safe remedy is to enclose interpolated values (package, package_name==package_version, wheel_url, onnxruntime) in double quotes when building the pip command strings handed to launch.run_pip. This prevents '>' and similar shell metacharacters from being treated as shell operators and creating unexpected files. The only file that needs modification is install.py; update the four functions identified to use quoted arguments in their pip commands. A broader alternative would be to change launch.run_pip to execute pip without invoking a shell, but that is out of scope for this PR.

agents.name: context_planner_agent
agents.system_prompt:
You are a Context Planner Agent specialized in step plan generation.

## Your Task
You receive from the Analysis Agent:
- PR title and body
- Optimized MASCA
- List of files to modify
- List of functions to modify

You must:
1. **Retrieve context**: Use tools to read the context files of the identified functions
2. **Analyze dependencies**: Understand how the functions interact
3. **Generate step plan**: Create a detailed step-by-step plan

## Available Tools
- `read_context_file(file_path)`: Read a pre-generated context file
- `list_context_files(directory, pattern)`: List available context files
- `read_call_graph(section)`: Read information from the call graph

## Required Output
You must produce a `step_plan` with:
- `summary`: 1-2 sentence summary of what the PR does
- `steps`: Ordered list of steps, each with:
  - `operation`: What to do (e.g., "Add validation method")
  - `file_to_modify`: Relative path of the file to modify (e.g., "src/module.py")
  - `function_to_modify`: Name of the function to modify (e.g., "ClassName.method_name")
  - `reason`: Technical motivation
  - `side_effects`: Potential impacts

## IMPORTANT RULE for function_to_modify
- If the file to modify is a Python file (.py), specify the function/method to modify
- If the file is NOT a Python file (e.g., .js, .html, .css, .json, .yaml, .md, etc.),
  the value of `function_to_modify` MUST be null

## Guidelines for Steps
- Each step must be atomic and implementable
- The order of steps must be logical (dependencies first)
- For Python files: always specify the function in the format "FunctionName" or "ClassName.method_name"
- For non-Python files: function_to_modify must be null
- The side_effects should mention tests, dependents, and API impacts
- You MUST generate a number of step EQUAL to the number of the python functions you are intendeed to modify nomore neither less

## Step Examples

### For Python files:
```json
{
  "operation": "Add time_spanned_alpha method",
  "file_to_modify": "manimlib/animation/animation.py",
  "function_to_modify": "Animation.time_spanned_alpha",
  "reason": "To compute animation progress based on actual elapsed time",
  "side_effects": "May require downstream animations to call this method"
}
```

### For non-Python files:
```json
{
  "operation": "Update API endpoint configuration",
  "file_to_modify": "config/routes.yaml",
  "function_to_modify": null,
  "reason": "Add new route for the authentication endpoint",
  "side_effects": "Requires server restart to apply changes"
}
```
agents.input_prompt:
Generate an implementation plan for this PR:

## PR Title
Fix issue with > in pip cmd causing file creation

## PR Body
### Problem Summary

When starting the Web UI, the `install.py` script reads `requirements.txt`. If a package version uses ">", it is treated as a redirection in `pip install`, creating an unintended file.

### Steps to Reproduce

Running `webui.bat` or `webui.sh` creates a file named "=4.8.0" due to "opencv-python>=4.8.0" in `requirements.txt` being misinterpreted.

### Fix

I enclosed the package and version specification in double quotes in `install.py`.

## MASCA Analysis (optimized)
Goal: Prevent shell redirection when install.py constructs pip commands by ensuring any interpolated package/version/wheel strings are quoted (so characters like '>' are not interpreted by the shell).

Key observations from the codebase:
- install.py reads requirements.txt via install_requirements(main_req_file) and builds pip commands using f-strings such as:
  - f"install -U {package}"
  - f"install {package}"
  - f"install {package_name}=={package_version}"
- Other places building pip commands: install_onnxruntime(), try_install_from_wheel(), try_install_insight_face().
- launch.run_pip is the executor for these command strings (implementation not in this extension). It appears to accept command strings and likely invokes pip in a shell context; therefore shell metacharacters in interpolated values can cause issues.

Minimal safe fix (scope of this PR): Modify install.py to quote any interpolated package or URL when building pip command strings. This keeps behavior identical for normal values but prevents shell redirection for strings containing '>', '<', spaces, or other metacharacters.

Exact modifications to make in install.py:
- install_requirements(): change all pip command constructions to put the package/specifier in double quotes, e.g.:
  - from f"install -U {package}" to f'install -U "{package}"'
  - from f"install {package_name}=={package_version}" to f'install "{package_name}=={package_version}"'
  - from f"install {package}" to f'install "{package}"'
- install_onnxruntime(): quote the chosen onnxruntime package name: f'install "{onnxruntime}"'
- try_install_from_wheel(): quote the wheel_url: f'install -U "{wheel_url}"'
- try_install_insight_face(): quote the wheel_url: f'install "{wheel_url}"'

Rationale: Quoting at the call sites is a low-risk, localized change that prevents shell interpretation of comparison operators (e.g., '>') coming from requirements.txt. A more robust but broader alternative would be to change launch.run_pip to accept an argv list and invoke subprocess without shell=True, but that's out of scope here.

Tests & verification:
- Run webui.bat / webui.sh before/after the change with requirements containing a specifier like "opencv-python>=4.8.0" and verify no file named "=4.8.0" is created.
- Confirm pip commands still install the intended packages (manual or CI test of install.py behavior).

Files to change: only install.py. Functions to change: install_requirements, install_onnxruntime, try_install_from_wheel, try_install_insight_face.

This is a minimal, targeted fix that addresses the reported reproduction path.

## Files to Modify
- install.py: This module reads requirements.txt and builds pip install command strings with f-strings. Unquoted package/version strings containing '>' are interpreted by the shell as redirections, causing unintended file creation. Applying quoting here prevents shell interpretation.

## Functions to Modify
- install_requirements (install.py): This function constructs pip commands from lines in requirements.txt (e.g., f"install -U {package}", f"install {package}", f"install {package_name}=={package_version}"). Quoting the package/specifier will prevent '>' or other shell metacharacters from being interpreted by the shell.
- install_onnxruntime (install.py): This function builds a pip command using the chosen onnxruntime package name (f'install {onnxruntime}'). While less likely to contain '>' operators, quoting keeps it safe and consistent.
- try_install_from_wheel (install.py): This function runs pip with a wheel URL via f"install -U {wheel_url}". Quoting the URL avoids shell interpretation of any special characters.
- try_install_insight_face (install.py): This function may call pip with a wheel URL (f"install {wheel_url}"). Quoting the wheel URL prevents shell metacharacter issues and matches the other fixes.

## Analysis Summary
The root cause is unquoted interpolation of package specifiers and URLs into pip command strings in install.py. The minimal and safe remedy is to enclose interpolated values (package, package_name==package_version, wheel_url, onnxruntime) in double quotes when building the pip command strings handed to launch.run_pip. This prevents '>' and similar shell metacharacters from being treated as shell operators and creating unexpected files. The only file that needs modification is install.py; update the four functions identified to use quoted arguments in their pip commands. A broader alternative would be to change launch.run_pip to execute pip without invoking a shell, but that is out of scope for this PR.

Use the context files to understand dependencies and generate a detailed step-by-step plan.

steps.operation: Quote interpolated package/specifier values in pip command strings
steps.reason: install_requirements builds pip command strings using f-strings like f"install -U {package}", f"install {package}", and f"install {package_name}=={package_version}". Unquoted values containing characters such as '>' are interpreted by the shell (causing redirection). Wrapping the interpolated package/specifier in double quotes (e.g. f'install -U "{package}"') prevents shell metacharacter interpretation while preserving the intended pip argument.
steps.side_effects: Minimal behavioral change: pip will receive the same package specifier but represented in quotes in the invoked shell. Tests that assert exact logged command strings may need updating. Downstream callers of install_requirements (if they inspect generated command strings) could see quoted args; otherwise no API change. Ensures no accidental filesystem redirections (fixes issue with files like "=4.8.0").

steps.operation: Quote the selected onnxruntime package name when building pip command
steps.reason: install_onnxruntime constructs a pip command using the chosen onnxruntime package name (e.g. f'install {onnxruntime}'). Quoting the variable (f'install "{onnxruntime}"') prevents any accidental shell interpretation if the package string contains special characters and keeps behavior consistent with install_requirements.
steps.side_effects: Pip receives the same package name in quotes; logging output may show quotes. Tests that depend on exact command string formatting need updating. Low risk to package installation. Consistent protection against shell metacharacters for onnxruntime installs.

steps.operation: Quote wheel URL when trying to install from a wheel
steps.reason: try_install_from_wheel uses a wheel_url in a pip command (e.g. f"install -U {wheel_url}"). URLs can contain characters that are meaningful to the shell. Quoting the wheel_url (f'install -U "{wheel_url}"') prevents shell interpretation of such characters and avoids unintended side effects (like file creation).
steps.side_effects: Pip will receive the wheel URL quoted; pip accepts quoted URLs when run via a shell. Logging and any tests checking command strings may need updates. Ensure launch.run_pip still executes correctly with quoted URLs — if launch.run_pip later changes to accept argv arrays, this client-side quoting will be redundant but not harmful.

steps.operation: Quote insight-face wheel URL when building pip command
steps.reason: try_install_insight_face constructs pip commands with wheel URLs (e.g. f"install {wheel_url}"). Quoting the wheel_url (f'install "{wheel_url}"') prevents shell metacharacter interpretation and matches the other fixes in the module for consistency.
steps.side_effects: Same as for try_install_from_wheel: quoted URLs in logs; tests asserting exact strings may require updates. Low risk to runtime behavior; ensures no accidental shell redirection or file creation when installing insight-face wheels.

step_plan.summary: Quote all interpolated package names, package==version specifiers, and wheel URLs in install.py pip command strings to prevent shell metacharacter interpretation (e.g., '>' causing file creation). The change is localized to install.py functions: install_requirements, install_onnxruntime, try_install_from_wheel, and try_install_insight_face.
